findings.title: Brief's no-PPE claim is contradicted by police and witness records
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment. Both the police report and witness statement say he was wearing required PPE, including a safety harness, and that the lanyard failed because the anchor point was part of the collapsed structure.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 4

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is direct and verbatim: the brief says Rivera lacked required PPE, while both the police report and witness statement say he wore a hard hat/harness and explain why the lanyard failed. This is a central liability fact, making the evidence highly probative.

findings.title: No-evidence assertion contradicted by witness safety evidence
findings.description: The brief says Rivera presented no evidence addressing safety, but the provided case file includes a plaintiff-side witness statement describing unsafe scaffold conditions, prior complaints, Donner’s dismissal of a base-plate concern, and Rivera’s compliance with PPE protocols. That evidence directly undercuts the “no evidence” assertion.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishes a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
I noticed several things that concerned me:
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
Carlos was doing his job and following safety protocols. The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is direct: the brief says there is no rebuttal evidence, while the witness statement provides specific facts about unsafe footing, ignored complaints, and Rivera’s safety compliance. The only caveat is procedural—whether the witness statement was formally “presented”—but it is in the supplied case file.

findings.title: Exclusive Apex-responsibility assertion is contradicted by Harmon foreman evidence
findings.description: The sources support that Apex employed Rivera, but they contradict the brief’s exclusive “Apex — not Harmon” framing. The police report and witness statement say Harmon foreman Ray Donner directed the crew to work on the east-side scaffolding and was told about, and dismissed, the base-plate safety issue.
findings.brief_location: III.A. Harmon Is Not Liable Under the Privette Doctrine

evidence.quote: Here, the undisputed facts establish that Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Employer:      Apex Staffing Solutions
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.

Tran further stated that she and other crew members had raised concerns about the condition of the east-side scaffolding earlier in the week, noting that some of the cross-braces "didn't look right" and that the base on the far end "was sitting on a piece of plywood that kept shifting."
Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief’s exclusive Apex-responsibility statement is directly undercut by quoted records showing Harmon foreman Donner directed the work and dismissed a known base-plate concern. The evidence also acknowledges Apex was Rivera’s employer, so the contradiction is to exclusivity rather than to Apex’s involvement.

findings.title: Time-barred heading contradicted by brief's own timeliness concession
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations. Filing on March 10, 2023 is within two years of either the brief's March 14, 2021 date or the source-supported March 12, 2021 date, so the time-barred framing is self-defeating absent a different accrual theory actually established in the brief.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is largely internal to the brief: the heading says time-barred while the body concedes the filing falls within the limitations window. Confidence is slightly reduced because the finding references the two-year limitations rule and accrual theory without the statute text being included in the evidence.

findings.title: Contractor-only control framing is undercut by Harmon foreman evidence
findings.description: Although this sentence is framed as doctrine, the brief uses it factually to assert contractor-only control in this case. The source documents undercut that framing by stating that Harmon’s foreman directed Rivera’s crew where to work and dismissed a safety concern about the base plate before the collapse.
findings.brief_location: III.A. Harmon Is Not Liable Under the Privette Doctrine

evidence.quote: the independent contractor â€” not the hirer â€” controls the manner and method of work performance and bears responsibility for workplace safety.
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.
Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
Ray Donner â€” the project foreman for Harmon Construction â€” told us that the east-side scaffolding section needed to be fully operational by end of day.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted records strongly undercut the brief’s factual framing by showing Harmon’s foreman directed work and dismissed a safety concern. Some interpretation is involved because the challenged brief sentence is phrased as doctrine rather than a pure factual assertion.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears doctored
findings.description: The quoted word “never” is materially too absolute. Privette is a real California Supreme Court case and is well known for barring peculiar-risk vicarious liability to a contractor’s injured employees, but it did not announce that a hirer can never be liable for work-related injuries to such employees. The quote turns a qualified doctrine into an absolute rule. Known actual language/rule: Privette held that the peculiar-risk doctrine does not provide a basis to impose vicarious tort liability on a hirer for injuries to an independent contractor’s employees who are covered by workers’ compensation. The Privette doctrine is not categorical immunity; later California cases recognize exceptions, such as retained control with affirmative contribution, provision of unsafe equipment, or concealed hazardous conditions.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The brief’s quoted absolute language is directly shown, and the non-categorical nature of Privette is canonical California doctrine. Confidence is capped at 0.85 because the actual Privette page text is not provided, so the determination rests on legal knowledge rather than side-by-side source text.

findings.title: Authority does not support absolute 'never liable' Privette proposition
findings.description: The quoted statement that a hirer is 'never liable' is not an accurate statement of California law and is very likely not a faithful quotation of Privette. The Privette doctrine creates a strong presumption/nonliability rule, but California recognizes exceptions, including retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazards, and limits addressed in cases such as Hooker, Kinsman, McKown, and Seabright.

evidence.quote: Id. at 702

findings.confidence_reasoning: The doctrinal point is strong because Privette is not categorical immunity and recognized exceptions are well established. This is marked as a duplicate because finding-13 identifies the same Privette 'never liable' defect with the actual quoted language, making it the more complete finding.

findings.title: Seabright does not support an OSHA-compliance presumption of due care
findings.description: Seabright does not stand for the broad proposition that statutory-safety compliance is 'highly probative' of due care, nor that OSHA compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability. Its relevant holding is about delegation of workplace-safety duties to the contractor under the Privette doctrine, including regulatory duties. The brief's use of Seabright to support an OSHA-compliance presumption is materially off point.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011)

findings.confidence_reasoning: Seabright’s role in the Privette delegation line is well established, and it is materially different from a general OSHA-compliance presumption of due care. Confidence is capped because this rests on legal knowledge of the case rather than text of Seabright supplied in the record.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: The cited case could not be confirmed, and there are strong fabrication signals. The cited volume 887 F.2d is associated with the late 1980s rather than 1991, making the year/citation combination suspicious, and the quoted OSHA-compliance 'rebuttable presumption' rule is not a recognized Ninth Circuit/California negligence rule. Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort-law standards except insofar as it applies controlling federal law.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)

findings.confidence_reasoning: The year/volume mismatch and unfamiliar OSHA-presumption rule are concrete warning signs, but no external reporter record is provided to conclusively prove fabrication. The finding is stronger than mere nonrecognition but still not mechanically verified here.

findings.title: Brief overstates Privette v. Superior Court

judicial_memo: The brief contains substantial record and authority defects that bear on central issues. Most seriously, it asserts that Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, while both the police report and witness statement say he was wearing required PPE, including a safety harness, and that the lanyard failed because its anchor point was part of the collapsed structure. It also states Rivera presented no safety evidence, although the file includes a plaintiff-side witness statement describing unsafe scaffold conditions, prior complaints, Donner’s dismissal of a base-plate concern, and PPE compliance. The brief’s “Apex—not Harmon” framing is likewise undercut by records saying Harmon foreman Ray Donner directed the crew to the east-side scaffolding and dismissed the base-plate issue. The time-bar heading conflicts with the brief’s own concession that filing was within the limitations window. On authority, the Privette quotation appears too absolute, Seabright is used off point, and six cited authorities could not be located or confirmed in available sources.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that under California law a hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work, and that claims against the hirer are barred by this doctrine.

citations.quoted_text: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that the California Supreme Court held that a hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.full_citation: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that summary judgment is proper where the subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), as a leading California Supreme Court decision; the reporter, court, and year are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that an employee of an independent contractor generally may not recover from the hirer of the contractor under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries covered by workers' compensation. The doctrine was later developed into a presumption that the hirer delegates workplace-safety responsibility to the contractor, subject to recognized exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The citation supports the general California rule that a hirer is presumptively not liable for injuries to a contractor's employees arising from the contracted work. But saying the claims are categorically 'barred' overstates the rule: later California Supreme Court cases recognize exceptions, including retained control that affirmatively contributes to injury (Hooker), concealed preexisting hazards (Kinsman), and related limits on delegation.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: This is an id. citation to Privette, which is a real California Supreme Court case. I recognize the case, though I cannot verify the exact wording at page 702 without a database.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that a hirer is generally not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine to an independent contractor's employee injured in the course of the contracted work, largely because workers' compensation is the employee's remedy against the employer and it would be anomalous to impose broader derivative liability on the hirer.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The quoted statement that a hirer is 'never liable' is not an accurate statement of California law and is very likely not a faithful quotation of Privette. The Privette doctrine creates a strong presumption/nonliability rule, but California recognizes exceptions, including retained control with affirmative contribution, concealed hazards, and limits addressed in cases such as Hooker, Kinsman, McKown, and Seabright.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this case, and there are strong fabrication signals. The cited volume 887 F.2d is associated with the late 1980s rather than 1991, making the year/citation combination suspicious, and the quoted OSHA-compliance 'rebuttable presumption' rule is not a well-known Ninth Circuit/California negligence rule I recognize.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the existence of the cited authority, I cannot evaluate support. Substantively, the proposition is suspicious: OSHA/Cal-OSHA compliance may be relevant evidence in some contexts, but I do not know of a general California rule creating a rebuttable presumption of due care from OSHA compliance.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort-law standards except insofar as it applies controlling federal law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), as a real California Supreme Court Privette-line case; the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Seabright held that when a hirer retains an independent contractor, the hirer is generally presumed to delegate to the contractor its tort-law duty to provide a safe workplace, including duties imposed by Cal-OSHA regulations, absent a relevant exception such as retained control. The case rejected liability based merely on the hirer's alleged failure to ensure compliance with safety requirements that had been delegated to the contractor.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Seabright does not stand for the broad proposition that statutory-safety compliance is 'highly probative' of due care, nor that OSHA compliance insulates a hirer from tort liability. Its relevant holding is about delegation of workplace-safety duties to the contractor under the Privette doctrine, including regulatory duties. The brief's use of Seabright to support an OSHA-compliance presumption is materially off point.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a well-known California statute setting the limitations period for personal-injury actions.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for actions for assault, battery, injury to, or death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition that California personal-injury claims are subject to a two-year statute of limitations is supported. However, the surrounding brief's 'time-barred' argument appears internally weak: an injury on March 14, 2021 and a complaint filed March 10, 2023 falls within two years, assuming ordinary accrual on the injury date.
